i-sxV2-vvwEh.name: Site
i-sxV2-vvwEh.description: The complete web property or domain.

i-77amDzVdN2.name: Bubble
i-77amDzVdN2.description: A thematic cluster of related pages.

i-ziaRlWZKzo.name: Page
i-ziaRlWZKzo.description: A page is a complete, published document accessible under a unique URL. It represents a full piece of communication intended for end-users. A page must have one clear purpose and follow editorial, structural, and information architecture (IA) rules. Every page has a fixed position of navigation on the top and footer on the bottom. All the page content is placed in between.
i-ziaRlWZKzo.content:
## Composition model

Every page must include:  

| MANDATORY METADATA | DESCRIPTION |

| :---- | :---- |

| Page title (H1) | Maximum 60 characters |

| Meta description | Maximum 160 characters |

| Page URL | Clean, tight URLs, kebab-case path |

| Navigation | Must contain a path of navigation and indicate navigation level for the page: meganav, bubble or link from another page. |

| OpenGraph image | Indicate link to the page thumbnail, otherwise default image is used. |

  

A Page is structured into three mandatory vertical zones that appear in a fixed order: **Navigation → Content → Footer**. They appear consistently throughout the sites. Each of them has an individual structural role, restrictions and predefined behavior.

  

| AREA | POSITION | CONTAINS |

| :---- | :---- | :---- |

| Navigation | Top of the page | Navigation / Primary branding / Search |

| Content | Between header and footer | Content |

| Footer | Bottom of the page | Global utilities |

  

![Page.png](images/2323ce36-d541-4671-8b76-ac38a51b2c11.png)

i-yoJyVydxF2.name: Content layout
i-yoJyVydxF2.description: A Content layout defines how Sections are organized within the page.  It sets the narrative flow, structural rhythm, and hierarchy of information. Content layout can be either modular or templated.
i-yoJyVydxF2.content:
## **Composition model**

Content in the modular layout is composed from the ready Sections. In some (rare and business justified) use cases, specific sections can be custom-designed to cater to the communication priorities of the page and incorporated in the layout. Those tailor-made sections can only be built from available Blocks or UI components.

​

Templated layouts are designed to fulfill very specific communication goals, they can be composed from the combination of Sections, Blocks and UI components. Layout composition determines the storytelling rhythm, reading path, and editorial priorities for the Page. The image below shows possible layout combinations on a grid.

![Grid layout.png](images/b9a6d2c4-40e0-4b0f-a1db-773593d75f16.png)  

### **Types of Content Layouts:**

Depending on the Page type layout can be a:

1.  **Templated layouts (fixed sequence)**

For page families with consistent editorial requirements (such as case studies or blog articles) the Content Layout prescribes a fixed, repeatable sequence of Sections. These templates ensure alignment in storytelling, SEO behaviour, and conversion patterns across all Pages of that type.

3.  **Modular layouts (rules-driven composition)**

For more flexible or mixed-content pages, the Content Layout provides structural rules rather than a fixed sequence. Authors can compose the Page by stacking approved Sections in any order, as long as they follow mandatory rules defined in the layout’s documentation.

i-RO0xf8rGKJ.name: Section
i-RO0xf8rGKJ.description: A section is a cohesive content unit expressing one idea or purpose. It serves as the primary building block of a content layout, containing specific UI components in the predesigned order. Sections in Vanilla framework are built with patterns offered as [Jinja macros](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/templates/#macros), which are useful to you if you build sites using the [Jinja](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/) templating engine. These macros can help abstract away some of the complexity of Vanilla's HTML, making producing complex page layouts simpler and faster.
i-RO0xf8rGKJ.content:
## **Composition model:**

**Structure:**

The majority of the existing patterns consist of the three main elements that help organize the content visually into one topic.

1.  **Rule:** a horizontal divider line that visually separates the section from preceding content (exception: Hero and CTA section)
2.  **Title:** in most cases mandatory text heading that defines the section's topic and helps the user to quickly scan the content. Titles use either H2 or heading muted style for Section titles and H1 for Hero titles. This area can also house (in some rare cases) supporting text elements such as a Eyebrow (label) or Subtitle.
3.  **Body:** The body is limited by the individual pattern anatomy. It is populated by Blocks and UI components. It serves as the container for the content.

**Section building elements:**

The following elements are the constituents of a section:

1.  **Section title:** introduces the content, should be concise and on point. It is a mandatory element for the majority of sections. Styled as H2 or heading muted - depending on the section.
2.  **Description:** primary text element used for the body content of a section. Optional except Newsletter sign up section. Styled always as a paragraph text.
3.  **Blocks:** repeatable structures containing parallel content units.
4.  **Block title:** text acting as an introduction to a block content or serving as a category of elements listed in the block. Uses H4, H5 and less often H3 text styling.
5.  **CTA component:** functional UI component used to attract user attention and trigger an action or navigation. It is positioned at the bottom of the content it refers to.

![Section anatomy.png](images/fc3646ad-f07f-414a-9153-97109b05a9fd.png)

  

| SECTION | STATUS | TITLE | BLOCKS | ALLOWS | VARIANTS |

| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |

| Basic section | Stable | H2 | Code block

Logo block

Linked logo block | Section title, Subtitle, Label, Description, List, Video, Image, Linked title, Code block, Block title, CTA block, Button | 1 |

| Blog section | Stable | Heading muted | Article (blog) block | Section title, Block title, Description, Blog card,, Linked title, Link | 2 |

| CTA section | Stable | Not applicable | None | Link, Description, Linked title, Title, CTA block | 4 |

| Data spotlight | Stable | Heading muted | None | Section title,Block title, Description, Link, Stat, CTA block | 2 |

| Divided section | Stable | H2 | Data spotlight block | Section title, Linked title, Block title, Description, Image, Code block, List, Notification, Button | 1 |

| Equal heights | Stable | H2 | Divided block,

Code block | Image, Block title, Description, Link, CTA block, Button | 2 |

| Hero | Stable | H1 | Equal height block | Subtitle, Description, Image, CTA block, Button | 5 |

| Linked logo section | Stable | H2 | None | Image, Link, CTA block | 3 |

| Logo section | Stable | Heading muted | Linked logo block | Section title, Description, Logo standalone, Button | 1 |

| Newsletter signup | Stable | H2, H3 | Logo block | Section title, Description, CTA block, Input field, Checkbox | 4 |

| Pricing block | Stable | H2 | None | Block title, Label, Description, List, Link, Section title | 4 |

| Quote wrapper | Stable | Heading muted | Pricing option | Section title, Quote, Logo standalone, Image, Name and title, Link | 4 |

| Resources | Stable | H2 | Quote block | Linked title, Description, Image,Block title, Logo standalone, Author and date, Category text, Button | 3 |

| Rich horizontal list | Needs work | H2 | Article (resource) block | Section title, Description, List, Link, Logo standalone | 2 |

| Rich vertical list | Needs work | H2 | Logo block | Section title, Link, Logo standalone,

List, Description, CTA block, Button | 2 |

| Tab section | Needs work | H2 | Logo block | Tabs, Section title, Block title, Name and title, Code block, Author and date, Logo standalone, Image, Video, Notification, List, Link, Description,

Linked title, CTA block, Button | \\- |

| Text spotlight | Stable | Heading muted | Linked logo block,

Equal height block,

Article (blog) block,

Logo block,

Pricing option | List, Linked title | 2 |

| Tiered list | Stable | H2 | Text spotlight block | Block title, Description, Button | 1 |

  

![Patterns distribution.png](images/a71fcc32-a180-4de4-854e-f4d459e34432.png)

### **Content guidance:**

Use Sections to organize a narrative flow in the content units. Each Section should represent a single conceptual step in the user’s journey down the page. Use them to:

*   **To structure page content:** use Sections to divide a layout into logical topics.

**To build modular narratives:** use Sections to stack different content types (text, images, lists, forms) to tell a story and ensure visual separation between different parts of a page.

i-UbqAYcn-kL.name: Block
i-UbqAYcn-kL.description: Blocks are repeatable micro-structures that organize content within a section. They act as reusable compositions of content and UI components that function together as a single group. While Sections represent the "chapters" of a page's narrative, Blocks manage the local composition of parallel content units, such as a list of features, a grid of logos, or a set of pricing options.
i-UbqAYcn-kL.content:
## **Composition model:**

Block is a composition of 2-5 elements forming a repeatable layout that can be combined vertically or horizontally. Blocks are built from UI components.

i-J6KXahUgv8.name: UI component
i-J6KXahUgv8.description: A functional UI component from the design system.

i-pXsRJolrey.name: Content primitive
i-pXsRJolrey.description: The smallest meaningful authored content value.

i-5CX0qx0-ix.name: Web design principles
i-5CX0qx0-ix.description: These principles guide decision-making across marketing, web, and brand experiences. They provide a shared framework for clarity, coherence, and long-term maintainability of Canonical sites.

i-DlY4iCeiNo.name: Content guide
i-DlY4iCeiNo.description: The Content guide establishes the editorial and structural standards for creating web content within the Canonical design system. While the [Copy style guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1AX-kSNztuAmShEoohe8L3LNLRnSKF7I0qkZGNeoGOok/edit?tab=t.0) addresses grammar, voice, and tone, this guide focuses on the strategic application and structural optimization of the page content.
i-DlY4iCeiNo.content:
## **Composition model**

Effective content anatomy mirrors the user's mental model. It starts with a single, clear topic (the Page), structures a narrative journey (the Content layout), breaks that narrative into digestible chapters (Sections), and finally composes those chapters using specific building blocks.

**1\. Page - topic or concept**

The page is a complete, published document accessible under a unique URL. It represents a full piece of communication, a source for a specific concept or search intent.

*   **Purpose:** a page must have a clear purpose.
*   **Scope:** subject should be narrow enough to deliver a clear narrative journey to the end-user and broad enough to justify a unique URL.
*   **Identity:** every page is defined by a unique title (max 60 characters) and a meta description (max 160 characters) that establishes its context within the site.

**2\. Content layout  - narrative**

The layout defines the storytelling rhythm. It serves as the narrative flow that guides the user from the introduction to the conclusion (or conversion).

*   **Flow:** the order of content must always form a consistent narrative communicated with clear intent.
*   **Structure:** whether using a templated layout (for fixed needs like Case Studies) or a modular layout, the structure determines the hierarchy of information.
*   **Guidance:** layouts prioritize critical information at the top and maintain visual calmness to help users process information without clutter.

**3\. Sections  - chapters**

Sections are the cohesive units of the layout, each expressing an idea, concept or purpose.

*   **Separation:** sections break the narrative into logical steps (see available sections’ documentation for more information). Sections are visually separated by a horizontal rule to signal a change in topic to the user.
*   **Introduction:** every section must be introduced by a concise title (H2 or Heading Muted) that clearly defines the topic.

**4\. Blocks - grouping**

Blocks are repeatable micro-layouts that organize content within a section. They act as reusable compositions of UI elements.

*   **Organization:** blocks allow for parallel content units (such as a list of features, a grid of logos, or a set of pricing options) to be structured consistently.
*   **Composition:** a block is typically composed of a title (H3, H4, or H5) and associated primitives/components (text, image, link) that function together as a single group.
*   **Usage:** Blocks are used to present multiple related items side-by-side or in a sequence without breaking the flow of the main section topic.

**5\. UI artifacts - building elements**

Sites use UI artifacts from the Global and Sites tiers of the Canonical Design System to implement interface design. These artifacts are the fundamental building blocks used to populate sections and content blocks, guiding users through the narrative flow and enabling meaningful actions.

The most commonly used artifacts on Sites fall into three categories:

A) Textual

*   *   Headings: these act as signposts that outline the narrative structure. All headings must use a sentence case.
    *   Body Text: the primary vehicle for explanation or storytelling. Paragraphs must be concise, avoiding long, dense blocks of text. Sentences should average between 12 and 20 words.
    *   Microcopy: supporting text such as eyebrows (labels) or subtitles that provide context or categorization above or below headings. Should be short and precise.

B) Visual

*   *   Photos and illustrations: visuals used to support the narrative or explain complex topics. They must adhere to specific aspect ratios and be approved by the brand team or used directly from Canonical assets manager.
    *   Videos: time-based media used to demonstrate features or tell stories dynamically.
    *   Diagrams and logos: functional visuals for data visualization or brand association.

C) Interactive

*   *   CTAs (Call to Action): functional components (buttons) used to trigger an action. They must be clear, compelling, and aligned to the grid column of the content they relate to. They cannot be overused or compete for users' attention. Only one primary CTA should be visible at once to the user.
    *   Links: text elements directing navigation. Links must be descriptive (e.g., "Learn more about Ubuntu") rather than generic (e.g., "Read more").  
        

![Content guide.png](images/ce7cadd3-3ca9-4d1f-880c-fd84d0431d48.png)

i-MughqPHCHW.name: Color
i-MughqPHCHW.description: Sites are using a predefined set of colors from the Design System palette. Color layering is also following a set of functional rules to ensure we apply the same visual choices across all our pages.

i-KFif7gGk3u.name: Spacing
i-KFif7gGk3u.description: Sites are using predefined vertical spacing tokens ensuring content is properly positioned on the baseline grid. All levels of architectural elementsv have a set of predefined spacing tokens allowing to maintain vertical consistency.
i-KFif7gGk3u.content:
## **Composition model**

  

**Padding values:**

Vertical spacing on a page follows consistent rules and has predefined padding values:

*   deep (128 px)
*   regular (64 px)
*   shallow (24 px)
*   none (0 px)

  

**Vertical spacing:**

The padding is applied to content layout as follows:

1.  **Hero top padding (shallow - 24 px):** to ensure proper spacing between first section and top navigation shallow padding is used for the first element appearing on the page.
2.  **Spacing between sections regulated by the bottom padding (regular - 64 px):** each section is followed by 64 pixels high space separating visually sections from each other. This does not apply to the last section of the page (5).
3.  **Top padding (none - 0 px) :** regular sections do not use any spacing as a top padding. Hero section (1) and CTA sections (using deep top and bottom padding) are the only exceptions.  
4.  **Blocks and UI components padding:** UI elements and blocks use baked in padding to ensure vertical spacing control between them.

**Last section bottom padding (deep - 128 px):** last section on the page is followed by bigger spacing to ensure proper visual balance of the content layout distribution on the page.

![Spacing.png](images/0a339fbc-c99e-4fc7-b540-777ffc25c91e.png)

i-T9g6Adkwpp.name: Intentional friction
i-T9g6Adkwpp.description:
Design friction is the intentional use of obstacles in a user interface to prevent accidental or destructive mistakes. It should be used when a user is about to perform an action that is irreversible, such as deleting data or changing permanent system settings.

For high-risk tasks, use blocking friction like requiring the user to type a confirmation word or check a box acknowledging specific risks. For medium-risk or advanced settings, use non-blocking friction like warning dialogs or technical language to ensure the user is acting deliberately rather than clicking through mindlessly.
i-T9g6Adkwpp.content:
It is sometimes desirable to introduce some friction in flows so users are fully aware of risks and take any irreversible or destructive actions knowingly. This friction is not supposed to be a substantial nuisance that keeps the user from acting deliberately, but instead a guardrail to prevent accidental interactions.

​For instance, a user using a wizard may be tempted to click repeatedly on the “Next” button. This may be OK in many cases. For instance, the defaults for the Ubuntu installer may work well enough when setting up a virtual machine. However, you may decide to disable that “Next” button in critical steps to prevent the user from taking destructive actions (like wiping a drive or not saving their recovery key) without explicitly acknowledging the risks (losing their data).

### Blocking friction

Adding friction that blocks the user from continuing the flow unless taking a very deliberate action is ideal for **destructive** actions.

For destructive actions, you may:

*   **Ask the user to type a word:** you may ask the user to type either the name of the action (e.g. “delete”) or the name of the object that will be destroyed (e.g. a VM or repository name).

  

![image.png](images/5baee484-0181-4254-ba8e-0a227c816946.png)

​

For _potentially_ destructive actions, you may:

*   **Ask the user to acknowledge risk**: similar to the way we collect legal consent, it should be voiced in first tense, e.g. “I understand the risk”, ideally explaining the risk (e.g. “I understand all my data may get lost”. This is a well established pattern in registration forms, so we can expect users to understand what it conveys.
*   **Ask the user to confirm they have taken precautions**: if there is any action that the user can take to avoid the actions becoming destructive (e.g. “I have recovery keys for all my encrypted disks”)

  

![image.png](images/9b37e8fa-6595-4dc7-ba19-ab7ec066affc.png)

### Non-blocking friction

Non-blocking callouts are ideal for **mildly problematic or irreversible, non-destructive** actions. Example: FIPS is an advanced option in Ubuntu Pro that facilitates compliance with government standards, so it’s only useful for a minority of users. Once enabled, it cannot be disabled and Livepatch is permanently disabled.

​

In these cases, you may:

*   **Add a dialog to confirm the action**: It will add one extra step to the process, and the distinctive design will help grab the user's attention, but it will only add one extra click.

  

![image.png](images/f863460d-dd43-4871-a6a7-418241c0202b.png)

​

*   **Show a warning message**: May be good enough for irreversible, non-destructive actions
*   *   Use [Notification](wiki-ref://grid-ymtdOF6n4R/i-LYZ4XyOt-O) in Vanilla or warning info panel in Yaru.

  

![image.png](images/262544f9-5048-4a5f-bd1b-561e6561d601.png)

​You may also rely on **obscurity**. If an action is only supposed to be used by advanced users that know what they are doing, or in very specific circumstances, you may choose to make the option less visible, or to use technical language that will discourage users from playing with it. This will force users to be more deliberate, perhaps by relying on fully-fledged documentation that can provide full context.

i-N9wKI9QSCL.name: Form placement
i-N9wKI9QSCL.description: Form placement refers to the deliberate choice of surface: page, side panel, or modal, for presenting forms and multi-input tasks. The right surface depends on the complexity of the task, the amount of context the user needs from the current screen, and the level of disruption that is acceptable.
i-N9wKI9QSCL.content:
### Quick reference

| Use Case | Page | Side Panel | Modal Window |  
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |  
| Process complexity | High | Low to medium | Very low (single action) |  
| Need for page context | No | Yes | No |  
| Form input length/detail | Long or complex | Short or moderate | Very short or single field |  
| User attention required | High | Medium | High (briefly) |  
| Best for | Full creation flows, settings | Inline creation, quick edits | Confirmations, warnings, deletions |  
| Avoid for | Simple edits or confirmations | Long, multi-step processes | Full forms or complex interactions |

### Pages

Use a dedicated page when the form involves a complex, multi-step process that requires significant user attention and multiple [Input](wiki-ref://grid-ymtdOF6n4R/i-9t3OC1fOBH) or interactions. Pages are appropriate when the content cannot be comfortably contained within a side panel, and when the user does not need to retain context from the previous screen. For example, creating or managing an item independently of where it will eventually appear.

### Side panels

Use a side panel when maintaining context from the current page is important. If the user is performing a task directly related to the content or structure of the current page, such as creating a sub-item or editing an inline component. The side panel allows them to maintain that relationship without disruption. Side panels work best for lightweight or medium-complexity forms where having the surrounding information visible enhances the user's understanding or efficiency.

### Modal windows

Modal windows should generally not be used for forms. Since modals interrupt user flow and demand focus, they are better suited for confirmation dialogs, warnings, or critical decisions, particularly destructive actions that may involve a confirmation [Input](wiki-ref://grid-ymtdOF6n4R/i-9t3OC1fOBH) (such as typing "DELETE" to confirm an irreversible operation). Modals should not be used as a shortcut for tasks that are better suited to a full page or a contextual side panel.
